message.content: Remote Service Session Hijacking involves adversaries taking control of preexisting sessions with remote services like telnet, SSH, and RDP to move laterally within an environment. Unlike creating new sessions with valid credentials, this technique exploits already established sessions, allowing attackers to maintain continuous interaction with the service. The potential impact includes unauthorized access to sensitive data, execution of malicious commands, and further network compromise. Common exploitation methods involve intercepting session tokens or credentials, leveraging man-in-the-middle attacks, or exploiting vulnerabilities in session management. Mitigation strategies include enforcing strong session management policies, using multi-factor authentication, monitoring and logging remote access sessions, and implementing network segmentation to limit lateral movement. Regularly updating and patching remote access services can also reduce the risk of session hijacking.
